Name: [ORG] Submission to the House Standing Committee on Health Bill C-293, An Act respecting pandemic prevention and preparedness The job of Canada’s 2,800 chicken farmers is first and foremost to guarant
card_text: [ORG] Submission to the House Standing Committee on Health Bill C-293, An Act respecting pandemic prevention and preparedness The job of Canada’s 2,800 chicken farmers is first and foremost to guarantee fresh, safe chicken raised with care for Canadians. Every single day, Canadian chicken farmers monitor their flocks for bird comfort and signs of disease, while following strict biosecurity standards to keep pathogens out of the barn. The Raised by a Canadian Farmer On-Farm Food Safety Program is a mandatory, third-party audited program of which 100% of Canadian chicken farmers across every province are certified. This means that farmers follow program protocols for best practices in biosecurity and disease prevention on the farm. The Program has received federal, provincial, and territorial government recognition, including full recognition by the Canadian Food Inspection Agency. [ORG] ([ORG]) supports the notion of ensuring that the government and the Canadian public is prepared for a pandemic as outlined in Bill C-293, An Act respecting pandemic prevention and preparedness. Many of the elements and reporting initiatives outlined in Bill C-293 are seen to provide visibility on the level of preparedness and insight into initiatives that the government is undertaking in this area. While C-293 seeks pandemic prevention and preparedness, the Bill includes content which goes beyond being prepared for a pandemic, and includes a negative, biased angle towards animal husbandry. [ORG]’s concerns with C-293 relate to the type of language used to describe animal agriculture, the focus on agriculture in the context of antimicrobial resistance instead

Name: May 24, 2024
card_text: May 24, 2024 CUPW submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) On Bill C-64, An Act Respecting Pharmacare The [ORG] represents more than 55,000 workers in Canada, including letter carriers and inside workers in processing, delivery and retail counters at Canada Post, cleaners, couriers, EMS dispatchers, and other logistics workers at private-sector employers. Many of our members are covered by extended health care plans with drug benefits, while some members are not. In the name of all members, with and without existing drug benefits, we call on the government to pass Bill C-64 as a step toward full universal national pharmacare. We agree with the position of the Canadian Labour Congress, in particular, with the critical urgency of passing Bill C-64 in a speedy manner. Medicare stands as a landmark policy achievement in Canada, much cherished by the population, and envied by populations of many other nations. Universal Pharmacare, for which Bill C-64 provides a framework, may be equally as significant an achievement. Too many Canadians are making difficult choices between medical treatment and other necessities because of the high cost of medication. To socialize these costs to the extent possible is only right, according to the very same principles that apply to Universal Healthcare. Currently, employment-based prescription drug insurance is the norm, compromising the ability of workers to freely choose – or choose to leave – their employment, creating a barrier to equity in access to healthcare that replicates or amplifies equity challenges in the

Name: Brief submitted to: House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Bill C-64, An Act Respecting Pharmacare Submitted by:
card_text: Brief submitted to: House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Bill C-64, An Act Respecting Pharmacare Submitted by: Ms. Connie Côté, CEO [ORG] Ottawa May 23, 2024 Introduction The [ORG] ([ORG]) is pleased to make a submission to the Standing Committee on Health regarding Bill C-64, An Act respecting pharmacare. Health charities have a mission to improve the health of people affected by disease. We represent Canadians with a wide range of medical conditions, many of whom depend on prescription drugs to live longer, better and healthier lives. The aim of providing accessible, affordable and appropriate drug therapies to Canadians is laudable. Bill C-64, an Act respecting pharmacare, has been long awaited by our community and our intent is to provide input that will help to strengthen and advance the implementation of Canada’s first national pharmacare program. Information contained herein is a summary of recent recommendations that were supported by various [ORG] members as acknowledged in previous submissions from the Coalition on the topic of pharmacare. As there is diversity in how diseases are treated, the size of the patient population and the availability of treatments for various diseases, it is anticipated that many [ORG] members will also provide a disease specific submission to the Committee. Context Access to medicines is an important issue for our members and to the Canadians that they serve. Prescription drugs can manage conditions, cure disease(s), improve quality of life, shorten or prevent time spent in hospitals and reduce the demand for health care services,

Name: Written Submission for the Study of Bill C-64, An Act Respecting Pharmacare May 2024
card_text: Written Submission for the Study of Bill C-64, An Act Respecting Pharmacare May 2024 Chamber.ca @CdnChamberofCom May 17, 2024 Standing Committee on Health Sixth Floor, [CONTACT] House of Commons Ottawa ON [CONTACT] Via email: [CONTACT] Re: Written Submission in Response to Bill C-64, An Act Respecting Pharmacare The [ORG] welcomes the opportunity to provide written feedback to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health as part of their study of Bill C-64, An Act Respecting Pharmacare. All Canadians, regardless of age, income, or geographic location should have equitable access to prescription drug coverage. This will elevate public health in Canada, reduce strain on our overburdened healthcare system and support a healthy and productive workforce. The government’s goal to achieve universal coverage is laudable, but a single payer system is not only a complicated and costly proposition, it is unnecessary. A report1 from the Parliamentary Budget Officer (PBO) estimated that total drug expenditures under a comprehensive, single-payer plan would be $33.2 billion in its first full fiscal year of implementation, rising to $38.9 billion within three years. The same report estimated the incremental cost to the public sector at $13.4 billion after three years. The PBO’s latest report 2on Bill C-64 specifically, recognizes the critical role of employersponsored benefit plans in providing drug coverage. The report indicates the true cost of Bill C-64: $5.7 billion over 5 years. The report assumes that workplace benefit plan coverage would not be impacted by Bill C-64. Name: Quebec’s current staffing issues and working conditions in Montréal area hospitals are degrading. Many nurses are confined to last minute mandatory overtime and scheduling nightmares. Due to recent ho
card_text: Quebec’s current staffing issues and working conditions in Montréal area hospitals are degrading. Many nurses are confined to last minute mandatory overtime and scheduling nightmares. Due to recent hospital closures and provincial financing issues these employees have had to assume the burden of staffing issues. This is not the case in Ontario hospitals, where the nursing union is probably the strongest nationally. In the not so distant past I was scheduled for an urgent surgery. My surgery was delayed 4 times, often falling on weekend schedules where staff was incredibly limited. Often only 1 surgeon and a couple nurses present in the OR on the weekend and if a priority case arrives, ie life threatening issue, your surgery is delayed. These delays caused major issues in the recovery and treatment of my own physical trauma. What is the government doing to alleviate such issues across Canada; more specifically in the province of Quebec?

Name: [ORG]’ Brief to the Standing Committee on Health’s Study on Canada’s Health Workforce February 23, 2022 The [ORG] ([ORG]) is the national professional association for social
card_text: [ORG]’ Brief to the Standing Committee on Health’s Study on Canada’s Health Workforce February 23, 2022 The [ORG] ([ORG]) is the national professional association for social work in Canada. Composed of 10 provincial and territorial partner organizations, [ORG] has been the association voice for the profession since 1926. There are over 50,000 social workers in Canada providing a range of services to support health and mental health outcomes, while also addressing social determinants of health at the community, family, and individual level. Social workers are Canada’s largest group of mental health providers. As we know, the pandemic has and will continue to bring lasting health and mental health impacts for Canadians, and the need for responsive, accessible, and timely services has never been greater. Registered Social Workers (RSW) are trained professionals with a broad scope of practice, capable of offering many types of services and care, often at a lower cost than other professionals – and can, and should, play a key role in pandemic recovery. [ORG] is very pleased to see this important study taking place. The pandemic has caused, and exacerbated, many challenges for social workers across Canada. The most pressing of these issues can be defined as follows: 1) There is significant demand for more social workers in an array of settings, but data is lacking to pinpoint the exact needs—that healthcare workforce gap needs to be addressed for the benefit of Canadians’ health. Prior to COVID-19, social workers were already stretched too thin in their roles,

card_text: Written Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Children’s Health By [ORG] From The Psychology Foundation of Canada [CONTACT]. Recommendation 2: That the government provide funding to expand access to existing evidence-based early parenting programs proven to be essential to healthy childhood development and parent mental health. [ORG] (from The Psychology Foundation of Canada) is a national registered charity with a longstanding history of helping children and youth build a strong mental health foundation. We develop and deliver psychology and evidence-based programs that nurture resilience, giving children a solid mental health foundation before problems or issues begins. We are pleased to submit our comments on the importance of providing adequate funding and support to early parenting programs in Canada and would be pleased to be invited to present to your committee. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY In Canada, substantial investments have been committed to the national daycare strategy and this is just the beginning. Investing in early childhood requires investing in early parenting too, particularly given that close to 50% of infants do not participate in some type of outside childcare. However, support for quality parenting (or caregiving) programming remains chronically underfunded. Children develop in the context of relationships. Name: [ORG] Submission to the Health Committee’s Child Health Study
card_text: [ORG] Submission to the Health Committee’s Child Health Study [ORG], a cross-sector collaboration of 1,500 individuals and organizations who champion children’s health and well-being, is pleased to submit to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health’s study of children’s health. Children and youth have suffered tremendously throughout the pandemic, but the state of children’s health and well-being was worsening long prior to the emergence of COVID-19. In fact, the pre-pandemic 2020 UNICEF Report Card concluded that, though Canada ranked among the countries with the best economic, environmental and social conditions for growing up, it was ranked 30th out of 38 wealthy countries in the health and well-being of its children. As the pandemic unfolded, it became clear that children, youth and families were being affected in unpredictable and enduring ways. Recognizing the transformative chance to create better outcomes for children and youth, CIHR Institute of Human Development, Child and Youth Health (IHDCYH), Children's Healthcare Canada, UNICEF Canada and the Pediatric Chairs of Canada came together to design a pan-Canadian, cross-sector initiative to measurably improve the health and well-being of children, youth and families. Between September 2020 and March 2021, consultations were conducted, convening youth, parents, researchers, healthcare leaders, educators, advocates, policymakers, service providers, community and business leaders and others. The consultations revealed, resoundingly, that this moment of crisis offered an unmissable opportunity for action that should be guided by a commitment to interdependence, equity, collaboration, and youth and family participation. By committing to these four themes, Canada can better

Name: Written Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Children’s Health Submitted
card_text: Written Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Children’s Health Submitted Children’s Oral Health Although Canadians have high rates of dental visits, children continue to have high rates of oral disease. The prevalence of oral disease is even higher when looking at children from lower socioeconomic backgrounds and Indigenous children.* Dental decay and early childhood caries (ECC) are both important issues when looking at the oral health care of children in Canada. According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), tooth decay is the most prevalent chronic disease for children and is five times more common among children age 5 to 17 than asthma.* The 2010 Canadian Health Measures Survey (CHMS) reported 57% of 6 to11-year-olds have or have had at least one cavity, 59% of 12 to19-year-olds have or have had at least one cavity, and children age 6 to19 have, on average, 2.5 teeth affected by tooth decay.* ECC is defined as the presence of 1 or more decayed, missing, or filled tooth surfaces in any primary tooth in a child aged 71 months or younger (i.e., about age 6 or younger).* ECC is a multifactorial chronic disease, influenced by biomedical factors such as diet, the oral microbiome, tooth integrity and underlying social determinants of health. Although all children are at risk of being affected by ECC, those from lower socioeconomic backgrounds and Indigenous children are significantly more at risk. Studies show that children from disadvantaged urban communities are at high

Name: As a Canadian, I will always remember the harm our government caused during the COVID pandemic. It will forever be a dark cloud in our history. The playgrounds that were shutdown, fences put up. The m
study_title: Emergency Situation Facing Canadians in Light of the COVID-19 Pandemic
card_text: As a Canadian, I will always remember the harm our government caused during the COVID pandemic. It will forever be a dark cloud in our history. The playgrounds that were shutdown, fences put up. The masking that was forced on our children, despite data showing that the rate of transmission in children was low. How the entire country had to lockdown, to protect the vulnerable when we should’ve protected the vulnerable and allowed the rest of the country to continue living their lives. This was a virus that had a very high survival rate early on, and it got even higher as the Omicron variant emerged. As of June 12th, Ontario’s total deaths are 90.5 deaths per 100,000. That works out to 0.000905% (Ontario Public Health website). To think the risk of dying from this virus was so incredibly low, yet these lockdowns caused such harm in so many aspects of life – it’s truly devastating. Yes, sadly people passed away from this virus. The majority of the deaths in Canada were in the elderly or people that had multiple co-morbidities. This was not a virus that was showing to take out healthy people. Yet even with data to support this, we locked down our entire Country multiple times. We locked down our schools before we locked down our airports – how does that make sense if we knew the virus was coming from overseas? For our children, they were forced to stay home, away from their friends, away from

Name: From not being able to visit my own mother during the last year of her life, job loss, business restrictions, having health professionals apologize but admit to not speaking up for patients out of fea

card_text: From not being able to visit my own mother during the last year of her life, job loss, business restrictions, having health professionals apologize but admit to not speaking up for patients out of fear of losing there job because of union and government policy. I have no trust in our health care professionals outside of necessary and basic procedures. I feel almost every single MP failed our citizens by fearing to speak out and speak truthfully and represent our population at the risk of losing there own job or be criticized and “cancelled”. I am ashamed of our government. I feel it was an absolute failure and I have zero trust in our political systems to respect and follow our charter of rights. I cannot even fathom that there was and still is travel restrictions. The government allowed so called health professionals to make financial and business policy and allowed politicians to make health policy. I cannot describe the failure of the government in the last two years or think of a single success policy regarding the pandemic. I once was a liberal voter, that has changed and never will I vote liberal or NDP again until these MP’s take responsibility for the historic damage inflicted on our population.

card_text: To whom it may concern, My name is Melanie and up until December 27th, 2021, I had been a long term employee of over 11 years at a Long Term Care Home north of Toronto, ON. November 10th, 2021 was my final day of employment, as myself and several other of my co-workers were informed we no longer met the requirements of employment due to personal medical choices made. I am writing this letter to reveal the devastation and hardship myself and my co-workers have had to endure since the termination of our employment. First and foremost, I would like to open up and tell you about how this termination has impacted my life personally. Since December 27th, 2021 I have had the challenging job of advancing on with life as though I had interest in continuing to live it. I have lost my career as a nurse, my business providing advanced footcare to the seniors, and also have lost my ability to financially support myself, my family and make my mortgage payments. My sense of purpose in life is now questioned daily. I took great pride in being a nurse and helping our seniors. I had devoted my life and a great deal of my time into caring for our resident’s as this is what made me happy and fulfilled my heart. I now work a minimum wage job and am falling further and further behind financially. I am 31 years old and having to restart and reevaluate my

card_text: The mismanagement of the pandemic and the effects. Quite soon into the Covid pandemic the science provided very clear guidelines to who was vulnerable to Covid-19 severe outcomes. Shortly after the rollout of the vaccine science also provided very clear evidence that the vaccine although helpful to the vulnerable populations and preventing severe outcome of the individual- was not affecting transmission or the acquiring of COVID-19 to the vaccinated individual. The Canadian government refused to acknowledge the above facts and continued to roll out their vaccine mandates with a sweeping general stroke- not ever indicating those most at risk- over 60 and with comorbidities. This has led many people to be coerced into the vaccine (at the time of rolling out mandates the uptake was approx 60% of adults) or risk losing their job and livelihoods. The government also doubled down and made those refusing the vaccine prisoners in their own country. I am a 36 year old individual who worked in a hospital. I had my first dose then decided to wait to be guided by the science. I will not be getting my second dose- because I’m not at risk, and I also already had COVID-19 and recovered. Due to my personal health choices I am now a prisoner in my own ‘democratic’ country and I am unable to leave or travel freely. Name: We all wish we had a crystal ball to know when the pandemic will be over, or if it will ever end. Every pandemic comes with its own personality. Some diseases come and go like SARS in 2003, while othe
study_title: Emergency Situation Facing Canadians in Light of the COVID-19 Pandemic
card_text: We all wish we had a crystal ball to know when the pandemic will be over, or if it will ever end. Every pandemic comes with its own personality. Some diseases come and go like SARS in 2003, while others, like H1N1, are adapting and we are adapting to it. We learn to live with them. COVID-19 is here to stay in one form or another. This does not mean that we should just give up. In its current form, SARS-CoV-2, including all its variants, is still a pathogen that is not only fatal, but can also have lingering effects on those who catch it. This makes it all the more important to continue to do everything we can to stop its progress. It will take years to measure the real long-term impact of the disease on people who continue to have symptoms months after contracting it, as well as on our healthcare systems. Several scenarios are emerging as to how many waves there are and how long the pandemic will last. But whatever the scenario, it is likely that we will never be rid of COVID-19. The virus will wane over the years, but it will still be fatal to much of the older, immunosuppressed population, a bit like a form of influenza, but more severe. However, we must draw on the lessons learned from what we have experienced now to better prepare for subsequent waves and for a future pandemic. Continuing on from the analysis I started in

card_text: SUBMISSION TO THE STANDING COMMITTEE ON HEALTH EMERGENCY SITUATION FACING CANADIANS IN LIGHT OF THE COVID-19 PANDEMIC SQI DIAGNOSTICS SYSTEMS INC. APRIL 22, 2022 TORONTO ONTARIO CANADA [CONTACT] Introduction SQI Diagnostics Systems Inc. is a precision medicine company that discovers, develops, and commercializes innovative rapid diagnostic testing for healthcare providers and patients worldwide. The Company’s’ proprietary advanced diagnostics targets organ transplant, autoimmune disease and COVID-19 testing. The COVID-19 portfolio includes three tests not yet authorized by Health Canada: the COVID-19 EXACT Antibody Test, the RALIDx™ IL-6 Severity Triage Test and the COVID-19 RALI-fast™ IL-6 Severity Triage Point-of-Care (POC) Test. COVID-19 Patient Management Tool: RALI-Dx™ IL-6 Severity Triage Test The RALI-Dx™ IL-6 Severity Triage Test helps to quickly identify COVID-19 patients at highest risk of severe respiratory distress and in need of immediate care. The test is intended for use in a hospital environment to assess COVID-19 positive patients at risk of intubation with mechanical ventilation. This test has a turnaround time of less than an hour and will provide a relevant diagnostic result that may be incorporated into COVID-19 triage protocols. This test identifies at-risk patients and helps guide the clinical team in determining immediate supportive patient strategies. The RALI-Dx™ IL-6 Severity Triage Test is intended be used with COVID-19 patients to aid hospitals with triaging patients quickly, effectively, and accurately. This device was developed in Canada with Canadian Institutes of Health Research (CIHR) funding and has been tested in Canada, the US and Brazil with positive results. On November

card_text: OTTAWA OFFICE | BUREAU OTTAWA [CONTACT]. Suite 1005, Ottawa, ON [CONTACT] Canada doctorswithoutborders.ca medecinssansfrontieres.ca [ORG]/Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Study: Government's Advanced Purchase Agreement for Vaccines with Medicago December 06, 2023 [ORG]/Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) is one of the world’s leading independent emergency humanitarian medical organizations. For decades, we have witnessed people being denied treatment for neglected or deadly diseases because the medicines they need have been priced out of reach by private pharmaceutical companies or never made available in places where they live — even when those medicines were discovered using public funding, including money provided by the Government of Canada. This system cannot continue. In recent years, MSF has consistently called upon the Government of Canada to ensure that public funds are used to benefit public health over private profits. Canadians support taking action to improve access to the medicines and vaccines that our government is paying to develop. In October 2020, we wrote to then-Minister of Health, Patty Hajdu, and provided her with a copy of an MSF petition calling on the Canadian government to make the hundreds of millions of dollars of public funding it invests in medical research and development (R&D) each year conditional on recipients of those funds ensuring that the lifesaving medicines, vaccines, or other health technologies developed with it are affordable and globally accessible to patients who need them. This petition was signed by 91,206 people. To ensure that public funding, which supports research and development

Name: Brief Submission: Transforming the Response to BC’s Toxic drug crisis: A [ORG] Perspective Between April 2016 and April 2024, the toxic drug crisis has claimed over 14,500 lives in British Columbia (B
study_title: Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada
card_text: Brief Submission: Transforming the Response to BC’s Toxic drug crisis: A [ORG] Perspective Between April 2016 and April 2024, the toxic drug crisis has claimed over 14,500 lives in British Columbia (BC) (1). Despite the increase of various mainstream harm-reduction initiatives, First Nations Peoples have continued to experience disproportionate negative impacts, including toxic drug-poisoning events and deaths (2). In 2023, 17.8% of all deaths from toxic drugs in BC were First Nations, while we only represent 3.4% of the BC population (2,3). Prior to the toxic drug crisis being declared a public health emergency in 2016, the [ORG] ([ORG]) was already on the frontlines addressing this crisis, as substance use by First Nations people, along with its consequences, is related to the systemic inequities and health disparities caused by colonialism that the [ORG] works to change. We were the first health authority in BC to develop a “Framework for Action” to address the toxic drug crisis. Our role is to engage with communities and expand our public health response to the toxic drug crisis in collaboration with our health governance partners. This submission offers an overview of the toxic drug crisis from a First Nations perspective, emphasizing the unique challenges faced by First Nations communities and individuals, and addressing the negative impact of policies such as involuntary treatment and restricting decriminalization on First Nations communities. The [ORG] calls upon the Standing Commitee on Health (HESA) to include a First Nations perspective in its study on the opioid epidemic and toxic

Name: Memoirs - Annie - 40 years old - implanted in 2012 - withdrawn in 2019
card_text: Memoirs - Annie - 40 years old - implanted in 2012 - withdrawn in 2019 For me, everything started with the Enquête broadcast. At that time, I had been having lots of headaches for nearly two years. I cried a lot when I watched that show. Maybe that’s why I’m having these headaches. Then I had lots of questions: What will I look like if I have my implants removed? It must be very expensive. How will I be able to pay for that? And what if that’s not even the reason for my headaches? I buried my head in the sand for a few months. But the constant headaches convinced me I had to do something. Maybe these foreign bodies were poisoning my life! A lot of my friends with implants say they’re going to wait to see if something happens... OK, but once the problem has fully manifested itself, by the time you realize it might be your implants, it could be irreversible.... Do you wait for the brakes on your car to stop working before you change them? No, because you could have a serious accident! We should not put our lives in danger! Another comparison I like is that reusable water bottles in stores are BPA-free because apparently, drinking from plastic is not healthy.... Implants, plastic pouches (plus a ton of heavy metals) are in you 24/7! Mentor silicone implants 425/450cc implanted in 2012. I had them removed on October 24, 2019. Symptoms: - Headaches -

Name: Medical devices oversight committee (breast implants)] My submission on my breast implants From a dream to a hard reality! “Why didn’t God give me breasts? I don’t feel like a woman or desirable with
card_text: Medical devices oversight committee (breast implants)] My submission on my breast implants From a dream to a hard reality! “Why didn’t God give me breasts? I don’t feel like a woman or desirable with my small breasts. I swear that one day I will end up getting a pair!” These were the thoughts that ran through my mind every day, every time I looked in the mirror when I woke up. Consultation was the first step After several years of mental torture, thinking about it , and once I was sure I was done having babies, at the age of 31, I decided to see a surgeon to eventually get breast augmentation surgery. My partner was completely against it, but I wasn’t doing this for him. I was doing this for myself, to feel better and to gain self-confidence. So, early in 2008, I met with Dr. Marc Dufresne, a plastic surgeon, at his clinic in Chicoutimi. After a quick review of my medical records, he asked me why I wanted to have breast augmentation surgery and then conducted a physical examination of my chest. He also asked me what my expectations were of the aesthetic outcome. I told him that I wanted something natural and not too big because I played a lot of sports and engaged in physical activities and I didn’t want them to become uncomfortable. Name: [ORG] Brief to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Parliament of Canada: Cancer
card_text: [ORG] Brief to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Parliament of Canada: Cancer Prevention Among Young People Submitted by: Marie Laberge, PhD Jena Webb, PhD Sami Fettah, PhD Sabrina Gravel, PhD Joseph-Omer Dyer, PhD Sylvain Letscher, PhD Nadielda Pastor-Bédard, MSc erg. Hélène Sultan-Taïeb, PhD France Labrèche, PhD December 3, 2024 Introduction We are a team of researchers associated with the Canada Research Chair in Adolescent Work Disability Prevention led by Marie Laberge. For the past two years, we have been conducting research intended to develop a program for early prevention of occupational cancers among adolescents with low qualifications and several vulnerabilities. We are submitting this brief to encourage the government to apply the precautionary principle when making decisions related to exposure to potentially toxic materials and environments. Both external factors and lack of experience make it more difficult to limit exposure among young people, and applying the precautionary principle would protect their health. Our objective is to prevent occupational cancers early on. It is estimated that between 4% and 8% of cancers are work-related,1 but this figure is probably an underestimate due to the difficulty in establishing causal links.2 In 2011 in Canada, roughly $4.4 billion in medical care costs, lost productivity and loss of quality of life3 are attributable to occupational cancers. In 2010, research in France estimated that the cost of a particular group of cancers (lung, laryngeal, etc.) for the French government was between €960 million and €1.866 billion.4 More investment into prevention as soon

[ORG] ([ORG]) The [ORG] ([ORG]) is an independent grassroots organization working to make sustainable health a priority. Sustainable health is based on a long-term vision that not only offers care to all, but also ensures that Quebeckers can take preventive measures to stay healthy. Our organization advises, surveys, educates and mobilizes stakeholders and makes recommendations based on evidence, expert consensus, international experience and social acceptability. [CONTACT] | [ORG].org 5455, av. de Gaspé, Suite 200 Montréal, Quebec [CONTACT] BACKGROUND In its report Alcool au féminin (Alcohol use among women), [ORG] presented a picture of alcohol use among women in Quebec, based on scientific literature, information obtained from organizations and the public, surveys, and an analysis of the alcohol industry’s marketing strategies. This brief summarizes the report’s main findings and recommendations to encourage initiatives to reduce alcohol’s impact on women’s health. Name: Submission to the House of Commons Health Committee Study on Women’s Health [ORG] January 23, 2024
card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Health Committee Study on Women’s Health [ORG] January 23, 2024 Fertility Issues in Canada [ORG] ([ORG]) appreciates the opportunity to contribute to the Women's Health study conducted by the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health. As the leading national organization providing free support, public education, and advocacy for equitable access to fertility care in Canada, our submission underscores the critical need for funding in fertility treatments and reproductive assistance. While this addresses the challenges faced by Canadian women struggling with fertility, it is also applicable to men. By taking action on this front, we can provide much-needed support to those seeking to have children and ensure equitable access to vital fertility care across the country. The need for fertility care in Canada becomes evident when considering: • Canada's fertility rate has undergone a decline of 15% since 2010, signifying a pressing need to address the challenges faced by individuals and couples trying to conceive. • One in six Canadians experience fertility struggles at some point in their life, underscoring the prevalence and significance of this issue. • The cost of fertility care, ranging from approximately $10,000 to $20,000 per cycle of in vitro fertilization (IVF), is a substantial financial burden, and achieving successful outcomes often requires multiple cycles. • Beyond the medical perspective of infertility, members of the 2SLGBTQI+ community and single individuals also seek access to fertility care to fulfill their dreams of having children, further highlighting the diverse range of people

Name: First, before I get into it, I have to say: It's shocking you don't have more women on your committee. I'm a 39-year-old white cis woman from Montreal, Quebec currently living in the capital region. I
card_text: First, before I get into it, I have to say: It's shocking you don't have more women on your committee. I'm a 39-year-old white cis woman from Montreal, Quebec currently living in the capital region. I got my period when I was 11. After 24 years of persistently trying to get answers, I was diagnosed at the age of 35 with stage-four endometriosis and adenomyosis, with painful lesions on my uterus, ovaries, Fallopian tubes, cervix and bowel. I had a radical hysterectomy, which plunged me into sudden surgical menopause. Roughly one in 10 people assigned female at birth have endometriosis. It has been found in fetuses, premenstrual girls and postmenopausal people. It is a condition defined by the presence of endometriallike tissue outside of the uterus, creating lesions most commonly in the pelvic and abdominal region, and in some cases also in the lungs, brain, bladder, skin and other organs. There is no cure, and no scientific consensus on what causes it — not because it is exceptionally cryptic, but because funding is scant and medical researchers are not encouraged to study it. Currently, the way most people are treated is based on a 1920s definition of the disease that has been debunked in other parts of the world. Endometriosis is the leading cause of female infertility and costs Canadian society billions per year in healthcare costs, lost productivity, poor career prospects for sufferers and out-of-pocket paramedical and medical expenses. Many Canadians travel to other countries for surgery because it

Name: Brief to the 44th Canadian Parliament, 1st Session Committee on Women's Health Authored by Manvir Sandhu
card_text: Brief to the 44th Canadian Parliament, 1st Session Committee on Women's Health Authored by Manvir Sandhu Dear Members of the Committee, As we gather to discuss and enact legislation pertaining to the health and well-being of Canada's women, our commitment to inclusivity, equal opportunity, and the inherent dignity of every individual. This brief aims to provide concise yet comprehensive insights into the prevailing women's health issues in Canada and recommends specific amendments for our consideration. 1. Breast Cancer Breast cancer remains the most commonly diagnosed cancer among Canadian women, accounting for 25% of all new cases. There is an apparent necessity to ramp up efforts toward early detection and timely treatment. Recommendation: Increase funding for the Canadian Breast Cancer Screening Initiative and mandate coverage for regular mammograms for all women over the age of 40 by private and public health insurers. 2. Maternal Mortality While Canada enjoys one of the lowest maternal mortality rates globally, some communities, particularly Indigenous women, experience disproportionately higher rates. This disparity signals the need for a targeted approach. Recommendation: Allocate additional funding to the Canada Prenatal Nutrition Program, particularly in underserved regions. Strengthen cultural competence training for healthcare providers to address health disparities better. 3. Mental Health Canadian women report higher rates of mood and anxiety disorders than their male counterparts. Unfortunately, mental health issues remain underfunded and often misunderstood. Recommendation: Create a national strategy to increase funding and resources for women-specific mental health services, including trauma-informed care and support for victims of domestic violence.

Name: Brief to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Topic: How Agricultural Chemicals and Clinical Coding Gaps are Fueling Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) Executive Summary
card_text: Brief to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Topic: How Agricultural Chemicals and Clinical Coding Gaps are Fueling Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) Executive Summary Canada's strategy to fight Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) has two major blind spots. First, agricultural chemicals like glyphosate and neonicotinoids act as lowdose antimicrobials in the human gut. They force bacteria to adapt and become resistant to life-saving medical treatments. Health Canada's safety models miss this entirely because they evaluate systemic organ damage rather than localized gut bacteria disruption. Second, national data on AMR is fundamentally flawed. Tracking AMR carrier status (like MRSA) in hospitals is weighed down by administrative burdens and outdated records. We cannot fight an AMR crisis if our chemical safety laws ignore environmental root causes and our clinical data is incomplete. Part 1: The Chemical Blind Spot in the Gut Health Canada and the Pest Management Regulatory Agency (PMRA) calculate safety limits, such as the Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI), based on systemic toxicity—meaning they look for measurable damage to organs. This model ignores the human microbiome. Glyphosate is poorly absorbed by the human body; the vast majority remains in the gastrointestinal (GI) tract. The Target: Glyphosate targets the "shikimate pathway". Human cells do not have this pathway, but our essential gut bacteria do. The "Dilution Fallacy": PMRA calculates safety limits based on the weight of an entire human body. However, because the chemical pools in the gut, the concentration against the 200–500g microbiome is exponentially higher, meaning the calculation is flawed.

Name: Submission to the HESA Committee studying Canadian Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Pharmaceutical Sovereignty in Canada: The Case for Domestic Manufacturing of Insulin and
card_text: Submission to the HESA Committee studying Canadian Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Pharmaceutical Sovereignty in Canada: The Case for Domestic Manufacturing of Insulin and Essential Diabetes Medicines Submitted by [ORG] Pharmaceutical sovereignty refers to Canada’s ability to secure reliable and affordable access to essential medicines through domestic manufacturing, diversified supply chains, and strategic procurement. For the more than 4 million Canadians living with diabetes1, uninterrupted access to insulin, glucagon and other treatments are essential2. Pharmaceutical sovereignty is not about isolation; it is about resilience. For the people in Canada living with diabetes, resilience ensures uninterrupted access to life saving treatment regardless of global instability. Without domestic manufacturing capacity, Canada has no mechanism to prioritize its own population’s needs over international demand. In 2024, Canada faced significant shortages of injectable glucagon3, a life-saving treatment for severe hypoglycemia. Following product discontinuations and supply constraints from manufacturers, Health Canada authorized exceptional importation of an alternative product from Amphastar Pharmaceuticals based in the USA. [ORG] notes several key takeaways we observed as a result of the glucagon shortage, including: Heavy dependence on global manufacturers Limited domestic production of essential injectables Emergency importation is a short-term solution, not a long-term strategy 1 Diabetes in Canada: Backgrounder.
